office_does: One of 49 members of the state Senate, elected to a four-year term to write state law, adopt state budgets, set tax policy, and confirm gubernatorial appointments.
race_blurb: Two sitting Democratic legislators face each other: Rep. Cindy Ryu, in the House since 2011, is challenging two-term incumbent Sen. Jesse Salomon, calling him 'too moderate,' while Salomon calls himself a 'pragmatic progressive.' Republican first-time candidate Ira McBee runs on partisan balance in this heavily Democratic district.

candidates.summary: Two-term incumbent Democratic senator, former public defender and child welfare prosecutor, who chairs the Senate Local Government Committee and self-describes as a 'pragmatic progressive.'
highlights: Supports a 9.9% 'Millionaires Tax' on incomes over $1 million; passed ESSB 5794 closing corporate tax loopholes and expanded low-income tax rebates [S1][S2][S5]
Emphasizes auditing homelessness spending rather than simply increasing budgets, and addressing the Aurora corridor 'with compassion and accountability' [S1][S4]
Passed ESSB 5611 cutting permitting red tape to speed housing, while his pamphlet also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI-powered rent-setting [S1][S2][S6]
Banned ICE face coverings by law and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state [S1][S2]
Endorsement base of sitting officeholders — legislators, mayors, the Snohomish County Sheriff — while the local party organization backed his opponent [S1]

taxes.basis: Champions a 9.9% millionaires tax and has an enacted record of closing corporate tax loopholes while expanding low-income rebates.

spending.basis: Stresses auditing homelessness program spending over budget increases and pairs compassion with accountability, though he also backs major public investment packages.

housing.basis: Genuinely mixed: leads with supply expansion, permitting streamlining, and senior tax relief, but also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI rent-setting.

safety.basis: Mixed posture: calls for connecting people to mental health and addiction treatment, framed 'with compassion and accountability,' and carries the Snohomish County Sheriff's endorsement.

climate.basis: Supports a fracking ban, environmental restoration, and a transportation package his campaign calls among the state's greenest; no explicit carbon-pricing stance in the dossier.

experience.basis: Two-term incumbent with a committee chairmanship, prior JLARC chairmanship, seven years on the Shoreline council, and bills passed each session.

immigration.basis: Passed the ICE face-covering ban and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state against federal overreach.

tech.basis: Blocked AI-powered rent-setting and is working on algorithmic pricing regulation, but has no stated position on data-center subsidies.

candidates.summary: Eight-term Democratic state representative and former Shoreline mayor, giving up her House seat to challenge the incumbent senator from her own party as insufficiently progressive.
highlights: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax; campaigns on a fairer tax system asking the wealthiest to pay their share [S1][S2][S6]
Housing approach leans on mixed-income and social housing plus streamlined permitting and modular construction [S5][S6]
Wants aggressive AI regulation, a comprehensive state privacy law, and opposes data-center subsidies; passed a ban on AI deepfake likeness content [S4][S5][S6]
16 years in the House, chairing the Housing Committee for five years and now Technology, Economic Development & Veterans [S3][S6]
Holds the 32nd District Democrats' endorsement over the incumbent, plus the Washington State Labor Council and WEA [S1][S8]

taxes.basis: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax and campaigns on making the wealthiest pay their share.

spending.basis: Runs on fully funding public education and challenges the incumbent as 'too moderate,' positioning toward public investment over restraint.

housing.basis: Emphasizes mixed-income and social housing, though paired with permitting streamlining and modular construction.

climate.basis: Has backed climate initiatives and wants clean-air legislation targeting working-class communities and communities of color.

experience.basis: Longest legislative tenure in the race: in the House since 2011, five years chairing Housing, current committee chair, with bills signed into law.

tech.basis: Wants aggressive AI regulation and a comprehensive state privacy law, opposes data-center subsidies, and passed a deepfake-likeness ban.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, entrepreneur, and international humanitarian worker from Lynnwood/Edmonds, running on partisan balance against one-party control.
highlights: Attributes rising costs to regulatory burdens and government spending rather than revenue shortfalls [S2][S3]
Supports 'responsible budgeting' and reforming spending inefficiency [S1][S3]
Backs rehabilitation for first-time offenders paired with strengthened penalties for repeat offenders [S2][S3]
Supports parental involvement in education, transparency, and expanded skilled-trades pathways [S1][S2]
Has raised under $8,000, roughly one-twentieth of either Democratic opponent [S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Frames affordability as a spending-and-regulation problem, not a revenue shortfall; no specific tax proposals in the dossier.

spending.basis: Runs on responsible budgeting and reforming spending inefficiency as core planks.

safety.basis: Wants to restore safety on transit and in public spaces with stronger repeat-offender penalties, softened by rehabilitation for first-time offenders.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no governing record, running explicitly as balance against entrenched one-party control.

social.basis: Emphasizes parental involvement and transparency in education, with a background in church ministry and homeschool-cooperative instruction.
